channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Ex-Overwatch Director Reveals Western Survival Game The Legend of California
item.content:encoded:
Jeff Kaplan, the man behind hero shooter Overwatch, has announced The Legend of California, a Wild West-set survival shooter that's slated to enter Early Access sometime later this year.

According to the game's new official website (which also serves as the site for Kaplan's new studio Kintsugiyama), The Legend of California will allow you to "explore a vast, untamed frontier and forge your legacy" in "action-survival" gameplay with a tinge of first-person shooting.

The game, which will support both single-player and multiplayer, will feature exactly the kinds of things you've come to expect from games like this; you'll be able to hunt, gather resources, craft new items, and build your very own Wild West-era base. You'd better have a saloon in there or you won't attract no gunslingers, cowboy.

The Steam page for The Legend of California promises shooting that "rewards precision and skill", as well as "optional PvP" elements and "robust gathering and crafting systems". In short, it's another one of these games, but Kaplan's name could well give it some extra heft.

As for the setting, it could well be based on the state of California's namesake, a mythical island first immortalized in the epic 16th-century Castilian novel Las Sergas de Esplandián. Of course, you've already read it, so I don't need to give you a plot synopsis.

The Legend of California will also apparently feature "persistent multiplayer servers", and while you'll be able to play solo or alongside others on these servers, there's no mention of an offline mode, so I'd assume it's online or nothing, desperado.

Publishing duties on The Legend of California are being handled by ex-Blizzard exec Mike Morhaime's Dreamhaven, so the game represents a real reunion of former Blizzard talent.

Whether or not The Legend of California manages to make a splash when it launches remains to be seen. You can wishlist it right here on Steam if you want to keep abreast of its progress.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/jeff-kaplan-overwatch-the-legend-of-california
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: PEGI Rating Changes Will See Loot Box Games Classified Much Higher
item.content:encoded:
PEGI has announced changes to its rating system, and they're targeting games that feature FOMO-style battle pass mechanics, as well as randomized microtransactions like loot boxes.

The European ratings board told Eurogamer earlier today that games featuring "card pack systems, gacha systems [and] keys to unlock...random items", as well as other games featuring similar loot box-style microtransactions, will be classified at PEGI 16.

According to PEGI director general Dirk Bosmans, the changes are aimed at "clos[ing] the loopholes" being used by publishers in order to circumvent loot box laws and restrictions, so it looks as though games like EA Sports FC 26 (which still very much features loot boxes via its Ultimate Team mode) will soon face a higher rating.

Another change involves games or apps with in-app purchases and time- or quantity-limited systems, like Fortnite's battle passes, being reclassified at PEGI 12. 

Bosmans does say, however, that PEGI's new system will allow games to lower their 12 rating to a 7 if there's a toggle within the game to switch off paid transactions. It's an "experimental" initiative that the board intends to look at again in "a year from now".

No such concession exists for the new PEGI 16 rating when it comes to loot boxes, however. Bosmans says PEGI isn't ruling out implementing such a feature, but that "for the moment, that doesn't exist".

PEGI is also introducing an automatic 18 rating for games that don't allow chat restriction, and given just how toxic the world of gaming can be when it comes to online interactions, I'd imagine that won't come as much of a surprise.

PEGI's new ratings changes will come into effect from June this year, Bosmans says, and that the first games "under these new criteria" should begin appearing sometime around Gamescom, which typically takes place in August.

We'll have to wait and see whether the changes make a difference when it comes to publishers including loot boxes and other paid content systems in their games. Stay tuned for more.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/pegi-rating-changes-loot-boxes
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
